speaker: interviewer
text: Roll one, Silvia Martin. Roll one, Silvia Martin.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not Miriam Martin. Date is February 9, 1995. The survivor is Silvia Martin. I'm the interviewer, Miriam Rutiz. City is Hollywood, in California, in the United States of America. And the interview is being conducted in English. Please state your full name and the date that you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Silvia Martin. I was born October 1, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I want to hear a little bit about your family. Could you tell me your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Berthold Grohs. G-R-O-H-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adrienne, Adrienne, Lauter. L-A-U-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A twin sister, Elfriede, E-L-F-R-I-D-E. Elly for short. And an sister Kate. K-A-T-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much older was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years, four and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit about your home life when you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a very lovely apartment right in the heart of Vienna with a wonderful view of the church garden on one side and the Danube canal on the other, which, by the way, is never blue. My father's business was furniture. We had a factory, which I never got to see, and a retail store right in the heart of Vienna. And mutti-- mother-- did not work, was at home. We grew up with a French governess, a housekeeper, actually two languages-- German and French. Not too much in French, more German. And we had a very happy childhood, very, very happy. It didn't last very long. But what we had of it was wonderful. Lots of fun, lots of games. Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What games did you like to play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, my twin and I were competitive. We actually never played together, against each other. All the games boys played-- climbing trees, tearing up things, we were a bit rough. We were not sweet little girls, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- would you-- you were competitive. Were you close to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. There was a great competition going on. Because when I was born, I was not expected. I came 15 minutes late. And when I was born, they didn't know about there are going to be triplets or twins or anything like that. So I was a surprise. And when I was born, my parents were told by the doctors I wouldn't make it. I was too tiny. I was too sickly, you know, which means I got spoiled rotten. And I took advantage of it, which means my twin was  very jealous.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you take advantage of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway I could, whenever I could, and anyway I could. I had fits. I screamed. I yelled, which means we had a lot of fights. We were very close, but we had fights.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you with your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all-- you know, five years older, there is not much relationship. She was the grown up. She was a very good little girl. She never did anything wrong. I never understood how. I mean, she was always perfect. She never had any spots on her dresses. And she was perfect. She was lovely. She was lovely, but too calm for us, actually, much too calm, much too quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was school for you? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved school. I absolutely adored school. I came late. I couldn't get up in the morning. We never walked to school together, my twin and I. She always came first. And I then came 20-30 minutes late. But I loved school. I really did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you in the same classroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We were always in the same classroom. But we didn't sit together. I was always in front, because I was so tiny. I had to sit on my knees. Otherwise, I couldn't see the teacher. And she was behind. She was also a very good student, a very good student.

speaker: interviewer
text: What subjects did you like the best?

speaker: interviewee
text: Language, literature, geography, history. I hated math, didn't understand it. I hated algebra and all these dumb things and science. I wasn't good in science either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your school? Tell me about-- tell me how big it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: How big? You mean, in how many classes there were? I don't know. We had about, I would say, perhaps 20-25 children in a classroom. And I think it was an excellent school, excellent teacher. In the lower grade, you had the same teacher all the way up to, I think, the fourth grade. And then you had, know, according to whatever they taught, different teachers for different subjects.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were there Jewish children and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell you. It was not-- it was not a subject at all. Religious classes were much, much later on in high school. It was not compulsory. You could or you could not go for every denomination. But in the lower grades, I had no idea. I couldn't tell you whether the girls I played with or the boys I played with were Jewish or non-Jewish. It really didn't come up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in your home life? What about being Jewish at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. I didn't know that we were Jewish until I was seven. We celebrated Christmas at home, because it was pretty. Christmas trees were pretty. And Christmas with snow was pretty. It was not a religious thing for us at all. Religion didn't come into it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't about Jesus and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, my gosh. No, no, no. I didn't know Mr. Jesus at all. I have no idea who Jesus was or anything religious, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and so you never went to temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went to temple. I went to church, because we had one across from us. And we played hide and seek there. So it was not for religious things, but, you know, it's a good place to run around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your home life? Like, what was your relationship with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good, except that mutti was much stricter than my father. I mean, I got away with a lot any way. But when we were disciplined, it my mother who did the disciplining, not my father. There were no hittings, no beatings at home. My father didn't believe in it. None of us got ever any spanking. We were punished, but in other ways, never with spankings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out how I was Jewish when my father died, whom I adored and worshipped and loved and still do. He died one week after I our seventh birthday. And when we came to the funeral, we drove out in long, long limousines. And I remember I was sitting with a lady I didn't even know, and my twin. My mother was in another car. And we drove by a big, big gate. And the lady said, that's not for us. That's only for the Catholics. And we drove on to the next gate. And there, again, she said that's for us, that's only for the Protestants. And then we drove to the last, the third gate, which was definitely the smallest. And she said, that's for us, that's the Jewish cemetery. And that's how I found out that we were Jewish. But it didn't matter. It didn't mean anything to me at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I remembered is I wondered why we-- why weren't they all together, like people should be? Why are they separated? And what if, perhaps, there were three heavens, one for Catholics, one for Protestants, one for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't ask anybody about it. I just wondered. I didn't quite understand. And, again, religion was not an issue except that the marriage between my parents was incredibly-- I mean, there was lots of love in our house. And my mother never got over it. And when she lost my father, I remember her crying out that she cursed God, that there is no God. If he could do this to take papa away when she had three little children and leaving her alone, there can't be God.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this stuck this with me all when I grew up that there was no God. Because why should he take Papa, who was never sick a day his life, by the way? And he got actually killed, not by gun, not by knife, but by doctors. He was totally, totally-- they didn't know what was wrong with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the time they did find out, it was much too late, of course. So he had never had a cold. He was never ill in his life. And he was 40 years young, 41 years young. So this was a tremendous blow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then things changed at our house. But you see, you have to realize, I became a child actress at the age of four. And from then on, I was totally consumed by stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the life I led had nothing to do with politics. It had nothing to do religion. It was theater, theater, theater, theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was under contract all the time, played everything and anything, which I didn't understand at this time, of course. I didn't understand the classics, I didn't understand the modern. But it was fun. It was great fun. And I adored it. I absolutely adored it. Now, I was really not a very healthy child. My twin was healthy. My older sister was healthy, but I was not. And because of that and work very hard in the theater, I spend a lot of time in hospitals. I remember when my childhood was being either in a hospital or on stage. Or, either in the hospital or on stage. I missed a lot of school. But I made it up in the hospital. And the teachers would send me assignments. At one time, I spent three months in a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I never really knew. They said-- the doctor said that my growth was stumped. I didn't grow. I didn't grow at all. That's why I got to play children's part when I was no longer really a child. Then I developed a big, big lump on my-- this bone here-- I don't know. And the doctor said it was because I didn't grow. There was something wrong. And he described treatments, you know, when you go to the lab. And the nurse led me there for a minute. And left me there. And I ended up in the hospital with third degree burns. And there they discovered there was something wrong with my lungs, too. They didn't exactly say tuberculosis, which I didn't understand anyway at that time. But I was three months in a hospital. And then they wanted to send me, I thought, to Switzerland, to those wonderful sanitariums and all of that. But I ended up on the roof, on the roof of, you know, the flat roof with, I don't know, 40 other children. And there was pigeons on one side, and we on the other side. And I spent the whole winter up there with lots of fresh air and lots of food. The other thing is I hated food. I didn't need food. I hated food except for whipped cream and chestnuts, you know. But otherwise, it was really bribing me. One spoonful from mama, one from grandma, and so on, until I was actually grown up already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So food was also-- I didn't help very much with growing. My mother used to prepare special things. My-- my sister ate like, I mean, you know, both of them. I really didn't need food, which means I also didn't grow. I stayed small for a very, very long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, I had an incredible amount of energy, even so. I rehearsed. I worked. I studied. I rehearsed. I worked. I studied, did the school work. And, as I said, except for math, which was hopeless with me, totally hopeless. And the teacher understood. He called my mother in and said, forget it. I'm not calling you for Silvia. I'm calling for Elly. She knows. She's just too lazy. But Silvia doesn't have math in her head. She can't do it. Matter of fact, they let me read stories all the time. Because they figured that way at least one of them is going to, you know, say something. And the rest of the class is going to be quiet listening to the story. And the rest is going to make their homework.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Beautiful. I never knew my grandfather. My grandmother was my mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malvine. M-A-L-V-I-N-E. She was very beautiful and stone deaf. Now, my grandmother, I don't know whether she was kosher or not. I really don't know. But she cooked those funny things. She did not live with us. She refused to live with us. She lived alone by herself. And whenever we would come to her, she would cook all those-- those peculiar things, gefilte fish and something that quivered in us, speck and terrible things. And I got sick every single time. But my mother made me eat it. But I loved her. She was wonderful. And I had always the feeling that, even though she is deaf, she can hear us. She can always hear what we want or what we need. She used to come once a week. And there was a big, big, big situations, big game. She brought-- I don't know what you call it-- those old fashioned-- it's not a purse, it's like a [INAUDIBLE]. And she had all sorts of stuff in there. And on the bottom, she had the presents for us. And we knew what she brought us. And she knew that we knew. And yet, every week we played the same game. And then at the bottom came out rock candy, you know, this wonderful thing. And we would bite in it. And we would love it. And she said always [NON-ENGLISH]. The best comes last. Or, for the good things you've got to wait. So it's always on the bottom. She was wonderful, absolutely wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you? Was she in your life all growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was in my life. She died when I was 15. And it was very strange, because I didn't even know that she was ill. And I often thought perhaps she wasn't ill, just old. Because for someone 15, 76 is very old. So she was-- one day, she was just gone. I never thought she'd leave us. She was so much a part of us really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she would travel to you by train? Is that how--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really don't know how. I have no-- but it wasn't that far. You know, it's not New York City, Vienna. We lived in the first district. And she lived in the third. I guess-- I don't know-- taxi or tram, trolley, something like that. I really don't know. I just know that our housekeeper said, she should live with us, because it isn't right for a family not to live together. She wanted no part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about other relatives, cousins or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a lot of uncles-- a lot of-- brothers. He had a lot of brothers-- stepbrothers. I have no idea. I think his father was married a couple of times or something like this. And I know that we were very happy to have that many uncles. But you know what happened is that every so often one of them disappeared. And rumour had it that he had gone to a strange country called America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were always-- always referring to this black sheep of the family. And I used to wonder why would anybody travel so far to live with black sheep? Didn't understand what was going on. There was disappearing one and another one, and another one. And also, he had one real brother-- or two. And the rest were kind of stepbrothers. And did any of them celebrate any religious holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I really can't tell you, because the only way I know them is when they would come to visit at certain parties or something like this. And these were-- I don't know what occasions were, perhaps just dinner parties or just to drop in to visit. I can't tell you. I don't think so. I really don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother support you after your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dear. Oh, dead. Oh, dear. That was very difficult. At first, you must understand, I was very young. I mean, money didn't come into it then. I had no idea what is money.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know the money I earned, of course, came all to her. What should I have done with it? But I know that we had a very beautiful apartment. And every so often, things would disappear out of the apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, the grand piano, we had piano lessons. And first it was we couldn't go on studying, because Papa had died. And for one year, no music in the house. I don't know whether this was the reason or not. But I know we had a divan with a lot of beautiful Persian carpets. And they would disappear. And mutti would say, well, they've just been to the cleaner for spring cleaning. However, they never came back. So you know, the divan lived out all these days without the carpet. All the beautiful things disappeared slowly. However, I know that papa left-- first of all-- the business and, I think, insurances. So it was not a problem for a while. But also, my mother wanted to let the business go-- go on for Papa's sake. And she couldn't handle it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took a partner and which was a former business friend of my father. He knew all about furniture. So it made sense, except that this man was a crook. And he took my mother for every thing there was. I mean, one day he took the business, the factory, the money, and just disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We know-- I mean, he was very nice to Mama. He brought us gifts. And he brought mama gifts. And then he slowly, but surely, he would take all  the burden of mama. She didn't have to worry about bills or money. He worried about it, except he didn't worry about it. I mean, he really stole everything. The other thing is that when my father died, my mother went into this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] spiritual world. She went to seances, from one to the other. I had no idea what this was all about. I just know that she was never home. We needed desperately mutti, but she wasn't there. She was looking for Papa. And I mean, I don't know how much money she spent on this. She went from one seance to other. She always reminded me of a wounded animal. She looked so lost and hurt. And much later on, I was wondering, do spirits need money. They took so much. And it took a very, very long time before she got back to Earth. And then she tried-- she never told us. She never told us that we had no money any more. You didn't do this. She said that, let's say, you're temporarily out of funds. You don't say you're poor. And she kept the housekeeper. Well, the governess went, because we were too wild. They couldn't handle us. So I think they retired at an early age or something like that. But she kept up the expensive apartment. She kept up the front, all of these things, got involved in most incredible schemes. She got involved in selling ice cream. She got involved in-- oh, yes-- marriage counseling. That's right, marriage for-- for for-- with honorable intentions. And it was in our dining room. I remember the very first guy. He was also a red head and very pale and watery eyes. I was 15. And he was very rich. He had inherited coal mines, didn't know what to do with them. He wanted to marry me. I was 15. I told him to come back when I was 21. He didn't come back at all. Terrible schemes, the marriage counseling. And there was the ice cream factory. And then there were a few other things. I have no idea. I really don't know. I just know that that was a struggle, a great struggle. But she kept up the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were in the theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the theater constantly. I was under contract to a very, very good theater. I played beautiful parts. At 11, I had my first solo part. Well, not solo part-- I mean the lead. I played the lead in a play.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it was wonderful. It was-- oh, yes, it was called And I remember that I really, I had the first word. I had the last word. And mutti called it type casting. It was absolutely right for me. You must know I grew up in an era of the theater which was fabulous. I really got to study and to learn with greats, the really the great, great, great actors of that time. So it was very thrilling. I mean, I wouldn't have exchanged this childhood for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you study with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started later. As a child, the director of one of the theaters was Dr. Leo Strauss. He was a descendant-- direct descendant-- of The Waltz King Strauss, the Strauss family. And he kind took me under his wings, like-- like-- you know, protection-- artistically only. And he told mutti that as a child I should not study, because then I'd lose my natural-- you know, then it becomes too stilted or something like that. And it was good enough for me just to use my-- no, listen and use my natural talent. I started much, much later. When it became time, he said to my mother that now it's time for me not only to use my talent but to learn the craft. And I did. And I studied with the very, very best. There were certain schools in Vienna, like Max Reinhardt or the Academy, Professor Wieland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- well, I played all through. I started with-- well, I started dancing much sooner, of course. I started to study dance-- ballet, modern dance, all of it-- much sooner. But when it came to acting, I was 14 or 15, something like that. I started, know, speech, diction, music, singing, composition, the entire history of the theater. It wasn't like you become a star, and then you find out who Shakespeare is. You first found out who Shakespeare is and then. It was very hard work. And did-- they had big, big requirements. But it paid off, because at least you knew what you were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that part of your high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, no, no, no. It had nothing to do with the high school, not at all. The lower grade school and high school, like anywhere else, high school. Afterwards, you had a choice, like here, you want to go to college, university, or business school, whatever. And instead for me to have a regular college or university, I chose the Academy to full-time study my craft.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- when Hitler came into power, how did that change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it changed my life-- actually, the second time that I was aware, or became aware that I was Jewish and it started to matter a lot was at 15. I had a contract to play the lead in a movie in Berlin. And it was-- we were ready to leave. My mother and I should have gone, when we got a phone call to see somebody in a hotel. Something was wrong with the contract because I was a minor, something had to be changed. And I went. And there was a very fat movie mogul who opened the door. And he tore up my contract and said my contract was cancelled-- not the film-- only my contract, because I was Jewish. I didn't have-- and my mother was Jewish, and my father was Jewish. And I didn't have an Aryan grandmother. And I didn't even know what that meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got home, and I was absolutely livid. I was terribly upset and terribly furious. And I started screaming at my mother. And then a lot of questions came up which I should have asked before and never did. How come? What is going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother kept saying, well, it has nothing to do with us. This is happening in Germany, only in Germany. They don't mean us. They only mean the German Jews. Jews that could never happen here. We are a civilized nation and so forth and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, I asked her whether Jews believed in God. And she said, yes, of course. And I said, then why don't you? I remember that you said there was no God when Papa, you know, went to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, I was crazy that I made so much fuss about it. And that it had nothing to do with us here. And it's just as well. They are, you know, barbarians in Germany. And I would have other films.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know that I was thinking about a lot of things at that time. But I didn't understand why. Why? I mean, what is wrong? What had the German Jews done anything? No, no. They haven't done anything. So why don't it-- does God not like the German Jews? Or, is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, lot of questions, which my mother couldn't answer anyway. And she said-- because she had said all along, this is just a hoax, everybody. Everybody is laughing about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, are the Jews in Germany laughing? And she said, well, you know, you're blowing this all out of proportion. It would never happen here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in the year [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '36, something like that. And then I remember-- you know, Vienna likes to dance and to sing and be merry. And they kept dancing and singing were very merry. And whatever political mess, you know, took place behind the doors, nobody paid any attention to it. I didn't. My mother didn't. And nobody I knew did either. And it was still propaganda. A lot of propaganda. You heard rumors and more rumors and more rumors. But life went on in Vienna. Now, you have to realize as far as I'm concerned, Vienna was-- Austria was always anti-Semitic, but never obvious. It was just there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- not me. I had no dealings with it. I had no dealings with it. But you can have a sore on your hand and you learn to live with it. You don't pay attention to it. It's there, but you know it's there. It doesn't hurt. It doesn't bother you. It doesn't cost you any inconveniences, but it's there. It shouldn't be there, but it's there. So this is what it was with Austria, always. And then on-- it went on. I studied for the state exam. I worked. I studied. I worked. And again, I didn't see any Nazis in Vienna, nothing. For the time I was there, I didn't see any of it, none of it at all. And whatever-- if my mother knew more about it, she certainly wouldn't have told me. Even my sister would have been exposed to it, which I don't think she was. She wouldn't have told me, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I certainly didn't. I sat home brooding about Jewish. I didn't sit home brooding about the Nazis, because I wouldn't have understood who the hell are the Nazis. What is Nazi? What? What? It's something you can't really-- you can't focus on it. Were you ashamed?
